primary_topics: Electron Beam welding: magnetic field distortion
Seebeck effect in dissimilar-metal EB welding
Electron Beam vs. laser deposition / additive manufacturing precursors
Radiation shielding and activation limits in multi-MeV beams
Spare parts and long lead times for large castings (Navy ships)
Gaussian beam modes and diffraction spots in lasers
Fiber optics for laser delivery (Yoel Fink / OmniGuide reference)
Inclusion vaporization and weld metal refining (HY-80 case)
Heavy section EB welding: Japanese coal gasifier program
Limits of deep narrow welds: seam tracking, NDT, repair, end craters
Laser welding of aluminum: reflectivity, oscillating coupling, double-pane window frame case
American business culture critique: Kodak, Bethlehem Steel, Japan, France
cases_referenced: EB welding cooling rate study (Tom's student, 10^6 °C/s)
Japanese coal gasification pressure vessels (heavy section EB)
Navy relativistic particle beam weapons program ($0.25B, pre-1992)
Pratt and Whitney laser turbine disc buildup (~1976-77, ONR contract)
Wyoming gas plant heat exchanger failure (70-week lead time)
Naval Research Lab 25 kW laser welding of HY-80 (~1983)
Mitsubishi Heavy Industries 2-inch stainless spherical vessels
Illinois aluminum double-pane window frame plant (10 lasers, 300M ft/yr, ~1980)
Kodak / Fujifilm / Japanese government plant approval
George Eastman / Kodak Park / MIT 1917 financial rescue
editorial_flags: p1: '1,000 deep' weld depth unit ambiguous (microns most plausible) — bracketed [microns] for review
p18: 'Professor Yoel Fink [Joannopoulos? — see register]' — referent ambiguous between student and advisor; flagged for editor confirmation
notes: Final lecture of the Summer 2013 Fusion Welding series. Audio cuts off mid-sentence at ~52:11 as Tom calls a 5-minute break. The lecture follows Tom's standard pattern of finishing a technical comparison (EB vs laser, point 8 of a list) then drifting into broader industrial-history tirade (Japan, France, Kodak, Bethlehem Steel).
